data.title: Mind Gym Says It Is in Talks for Possible Takeover
data.description:
By Adria Calatayud

Mind Gym said its board is in talks with third parties that might lead to a possible takeover offer for the company.

"These discussions are at an early stage and there can be no certainty that an offer will be made for [the] company nor as to the terms on which any such offer might be made," the London-based consultancy said late Tuesday.

The company said its board is considering options including soliciting a takeover offer, selling parts of the business or making acquisitions as part of a strategic review.

As of Tuesday's close, Mind Gym had a market value of 12 million pounds ($16.6 million), according to FactSet.

Write to Adria Calatayud at adria.calatayud@wsj.com

data.title: Lonza Sales Grow on New Contract Momentum
data.description:
By Billy Gray

Lonza said sales grew in 2025 on the strength of new commercial contracts across technology programs.

The healthcare manufacturer and contract-development group on Wednesday reported 2025 sales of 6.53 billion Swiss francs, equivalent to $8.58 billion, up 19.2% compared with 2024. Analysts saw sales for the year at 7.54 billion francs, according to consensus estimates compiled by Vara Research.

Lonza highlighted contract activity at its biologics manufacturing site in Vacaville, Calif., as a reason for its solid performance.

The company reported 2025 core earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization of 2.06 billion francs, a 24.9% increase from 2024. Analysts had forecast core Ebitda of 2.27 billion francs, according to Vara Research.

Lonza said that in the current year it expects sales growth of 11% to 12% at constant currencies and further core Ebitda margin expansion, reaching a level above 32%.

Write to Billy Gray at william.gray@wsj.com

data.title: Dollar Likely to Trade at 150-158 Yen Near Term — Market Talk
data.description:
0616 GMT - The dollar will likely continue to trade in the 150-158 range against the yen in the near term, according to BofA Global Research. Reported rate checks by the U.S. could have been motivated by efforts to address yen undervaluation, supporting U.S. Treasurys, it adds. Japan may want to strike a balance to stabilize the currency, bond and equity markets, the research team writes in a note. That said, if conditions change suddenly and USD/JPY quickly reverts to prevailing levels over the past few weeks, any U.S. absence could pose effectiveness risks for future rate checks, it notes. USD/JPY is 0.3% higher at 152.63. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0550 GMT - With or without intervention, FX markets seemed long overdue for a course correction, says Stefan Angrick at Moody's Analytics. The yen and the won sit near multidecade lows, looking deeply undervalued relative to interest-rate differentials and broader fundamentals, he says. Currencies of Taiwan and China also trade cheaply relative to external balances. Tolerance for these distortions looks increasingly thin, Angrick says. Tokyo and Seoul have repeatedly expressed discomfort with currency weakness, while Trump sees USD strength as a drag on manufacturing. Latest market moves fit the narrative of an attempt to echo the 1985 Plaza Accord by engineering a weaker dollar to narrow the U.S. trade deficit. The violent swings serve as a reminder that once trading dynamics flip, deeply misaligned currencies rarely adjust smoothly. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0545 GMT - The Bank of Japan may raise its policy rate again in the April-June period, possibly outpacing the six-month cycle expected previously, Daiwa Securities economists say. "Fiscal expansion and the weak yen have fueled concerns over rising prices, prompting calls for an earlier interest-rate hike," the economists say in a research note. As BOJ Gov. Kazuo Ueda has signaled he will closely watch Japanese companies' price revisions in April, the central bank's branch managers' meeting that month and Tokyo CPI figures for April may hold the key to the next hike, they add. (megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com)

0530 GMT - The yen's dramatic weekend rally sparked speculation about official intervention but the usual early indicators of such action look quiet for now, says Stefan Angrick at Moody's Analytics. While Japan's finance ministry releases official intervention data with a lag, BOJ accounts and money broker estimates typically give reliable early clues. Such measures correctly flagged intervention in July 2024, and this time around, there are no such signs, the economist writes. Governments don't always need to pull the trigger to move markets--a credible threat of coordinated action can be enough, especially if Japan and the U.S. join forces, he says. The yen is last at 152.59 to the dollar, a ways off the 160 zone viewed as a trigger for intervention. (fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

0527 GMT - Possible joint U.S.-Japan intervention to weaken the dollar versus the yen would carry far more credibility that unilateral efforts by Japan, say OCBC strategists in a note. The Treasury Department last week took steps that sparked speculation that it could try to boost the Japanese currency versus the greenback. These actions have sharpened the market's focus on the two currencies, the strategists note. While the speculated move would be more effective than any solo moves by Japan, U.S. intervention could raise questions about whether U.S. policymakers are becoming more tolerant of a softer dollar, OCBC says. USD/JPY is 0.3% higher at 152.59. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0457 GMT - JGBs rise in price terms across the yield curve in the afternoon Tokyo session after the Japanese Finance Ministry's auction of 40-year sovereign debt drew strong demand. The bid-to-cover ratio, a measure of demand in bond auctions, stood at 2.76. The ratio was the highest since the 40-year sale held in March 2025 and higher than the 2.59 ratio at November's 40-year auction. A higher ratio indicates stronger demand. The 2-year JGB yield is down 2.5bps to 1.250%, the 10-year yield falls 3.5bps to 1.250%, and the 40-year yield is 2.5bps lower at 3.910%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0441 GMT - Gold climbs to a fresh record amid negative sentiment toward the U.S. dollar. President Trump said he wasn't worried about recent weakness in the U.S. currency. When asked Tuesday by a reporter in Iowa whether the dollar had recently fallen too much, Trump said, "No, I think it's great." There seems to be a "confluence of risks that are negative for the USD now," Maybank analysts say in a FX research & strategy report. Hence, gold and silver have climbed, the analysts add. A weaker U.S. dollar makes USD-denominated gold and silver cheaper for investors holding non-USD currencies. Spot gold is 1.1% higher at $5,235.37/oz after earlier touching $5,247.57/oz, ICE data show. Spot silver is up 2.9% at $115.38/oz.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0437 GMT - The Reserve Bank of Australia will take some political heat if it raises interest rates next week, with some already claiming it would be an embarrassing U-turn for the central bank, which lowered the official cash rate three times in 2025. Still, keen observers will understand that the RBA wasn't dealing with a broken economy when it started to ease last year. Instead, the job market was pretty resilient, and the economy was in an upswing. Given that backdrop, it was never going to be the case that cuts would be deep or that there was going to be an extended pause before tightening was required. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0405 GMT - Given the persistence in inflation in Australia and a better labor market than the Reserve Bank of Australia had anticipated, the central bank looks to be on track to raise the official cash rate next week. Still, Kieran Davies, economist at Coolabah Capital, says the RBA rarely hikes rates once, and a message of fine-tuning with only one rate rise would risk undermining the impact of tighter policy. So a follow-up hike in May seems likely, when the first-quarter CPI data will be known, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0400 GMT - The Reserve Bank of Australia now appears on track to raise the official cash rate by 25 basis points to 3.85% next week, but there is a strong argument that it might be a one-and-done for the central bank. Core inflation is clearly too high for now, but it might cool through the year, says Adelaide Timbrell, economist at ANZ. There are some signs of disinflation in the December CPI data, with the monthly trimmed mean at 0.2% on month, she says. It suggests that quarterly trimmed mean inflation may ease a little from here, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0351 GMT - The most important figure in Australia's 4Q 2025 CPI data was arguably the trimmed mean measure, as it has a longer history and the Reserve Bank of Australia has also indicated that it's critical. It was marginally higher than consensus at a rate of 0.9% over the quarter and 3.4% over the year, which higher than the RBA's forecast back in November for a 3.2% rise. My Bui, economist at AMP says this is too high for the RBA's liking. The upward trajectory in core inflation since June is also a worry, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0335 GMT - Ongoing persistence in Australian underlying inflation pressures will persuade the Reserve Bank of Australia to reverse course on rate cuts next month, says Abhijit Surya, economist at Capital Economics. He expects the RBA to raise rates by 25 basis points at its policy meeting next week, followed by another hike in May. That will be enough to stamp out excess demand in the economy, though markets remain wary about the possibility of further tightening, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; X @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Standard Chartered Expects Metal Prices To Remain Elevated This Year
data.description:
STANDARD CHARTERED:

  • SAYS IT EXPECTS BASE METALS PRICES TO REMAIN ELEVATED THIS YEAR, PARTICULARLY IN H1 2026, DRIVEN BY BOTH MACRO AND MICRO FACTORS

  • SAYS ON METALS, KEY RISKS FOR 2026 INCLUDE TARIFFS, SUPPLY AND THE MACRO BACKDROP

  • SAYS ON COPPER, IT FORECASTS AVERAGE H1 PRICES AT $12,950/T COMPARED WITH $11,475/T IN H2

  • SAYS USD SOFTNESS AND SHARP MOVES HIGHER IN GOLD AND SILVER HAVE SUPPORTED COPPER PRICES

  • SAYS REDUCED AVAILABILITY OF STOCK HAS TIGHTENED PRECIOUS METALS MARKETS, BUT ELEVATED PRICES PUT SILVER INDUSTRIAL DEMAND AT RISK

  • SAYS BOTH GOLD AND SILVER PRICES ARE TRADING IN OVERBOUGHT

    TERRITORY, BUT A NUMBER OF SILVER INDICATORS SUGGEST PRICES MAY BE DUE A CORRECTION IN THE SHORT TERM

data.title: Lonza forecasts 2026 sales growth of 11-12% for core CDMO business
data.description:
Lonza , the world's largest contract drug manufacturer, on Wednesday set 2026 targets for its contract development and manufacturing organization (CDMO) business, expecting sales growth of 11% to 12% at constant exchange rates and core profit margin expansion to above 32% of sales.

The Swiss company added it expected sales growth to be higher in the first half of 2026 than in the second half.

data.title: Dubai launches global challenge to build world’s first villa using robotic construction
data.description:
Staff Writer

DUBAI - Dubai Municipality has launched of a global challenge to construct the world’s first residential villa built entirely using robotic construction systems.

The project will be delivered through a local and international consortium led by Dubai Municipality and comprising more than 25 advanced technology companies and academic institutions.

The initiative aims to develop scalable models for future construction, improving productivity, sustainability and build quality, while positioning Dubai as a hub for advanced construction technologies.

The robotic villa project will be implemented in partnership with Zacua Ventures and the Würth Group, with the participation of construction robotics companies alongside local contractors and engineering firms.

The announcement was made during an event marking the activation of the Construction Innovation and Research Centre (04 ConTech Valley) in partnership with Expo City Dubai, following the signing of an agreement to establish a dedicated centre for innovation and research in construction materials, systems and technologies.

The centre aims to support the development of next-generation construction solutions, urban systems and future city infrastructure.

During the event, Dubai Municipality also launched the Global ConTech Report in collaboration with Zacua Ventures. The report analyses the accelerating global adoption of construction technologies and projects that investments in the sector will exceed $30 billion by 2033, with an annual growth rate of 17.5 percent.

The report identifies labour shortages as one of the most pressing challenges facing the global construction industry, driving increased investment in technologies such as robotics and additive manufacturing. It also highlights the most prominent technologies shaping the sector, including artificial intelligence, robotics, infrastructure technology and prefabrication.

Dubai Municipality also launched the ConTech Working Group, in collaboration with Dubai Chambers, bringing together government entities, developers, contractors, technology providers, investors and researchers to support innovation and efficiency across the construction sector.

As part of supporting this ecosystem, Dubai Municipality signed three cooperation agreements with Zacua Ventures, the Dubai Future District Fund and LAB Ventures to support startups by facilitating access to projects, strengthening engagement with the sector ecosystem, and enhancing research, development and investment in future technologies, while attracting construction technology companies from around the world to Dubai.

Marwan Ahmed bin Ghalita, Director-General of Dubai Municipality, said that the adoption of advanced construction technologies represents a cornerstone of Dubai’s vision to develop a more efficient, sustainable and resilient construction sector, guided by a comprehensive, future-focused construction technology strategy.

He added, “The activation of 04 ConTech Valley, the launch of the ConTech Working Group in collaboration with Dubai Chambers, the signing of agreements with capital partners and the announcement of the challenge to build the world’s first residential villa using robotic construction systems collectively underscore Dubai Municipality’s commitment to strengthening the innovation ecosystem within the construction sector.”

Dubai Municipality, in collaboration with Sobha Realty, also launched the 70–70 Strategy for 2030, which aims to shift 70 percent of construction to off-site manufacturing while achieving a minimum of 70 percent automation within factories by 2030, contributing to higher quality, efficiency and sustainability across the sector.

During the event, partners from the public and private sectors, alongside construction technology startups, discussed opportunities and challenges within the construction ecosystem and outlined a roadmap for construction technology development under Dubai Municipality’s leadership.

Dubai Municipality also accredited China State Construction Engineering Corporation for its modular construction system, marking a new milestone in smart construction standards and practices in Dubai. In addition, AMANA will adopt modular construction systems to develop shared workspaces for youth within public facilities, providing flexible and integrated working environments.

Article Source: Emirates News Agency (WAM)
